checks.a_analysis: The converted document faithfully transforms the slide-based layout of the source PDF into a linear, scrolling HTML format. The logical organization of information remains consistent; headers, text blocks, and associated visual placeholders follow the same sequence as the original slides. Information for each slide is contained within its own section, preserving the thematic grouping intended by the author.

The document does not contain traditional complex data tables. It utilizes grids for product galleries (Slide 17) and multi-column lists for glossaries (Slide 4). These are rendered in a readable and organized manner in the HTML output, allowing a user to understand the content structure. No overlapping content or missing borders in tabular data were observed.

Multiple meaningful images from the source PDF are broken or missing in the HTML output, rendered instead as solid green rectangular blocks. These missing images are critical to the content, including screenshots of the GIS tools (Worldview, Earthdata Search) and scientific data visualizations (NDVI maps, flood maps, and heat maps on Slide 17). This significantly impacts the informative value of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 17 contains several scientific maps: 'NDVI Change Detection', 'Classified Post-Event Flood Map', 'Landslide Proximity Heat Map', and 'Black Marble HD Night Lights Power Outage Map'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The maps and data visualizations are replaced by solid green rectangular placeholders in the rendered HTML.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly reflects the intentional repetition of the 'Discovery & Access Methods' slide which appears on pages 5, 9, and 13 of the source PDF. No other instances of unintentional duplication of body content or paragraphs were detected within the rendered document.
